topics.title: MySQL Query Optimization — EXPLAIN Plans & Slow Query Tuning
topics.description: Query optimization is the practice of rewriting SQL and designing schemas to minimize rows scanned and I/O operations. EXPLAIN reveals the query execution plan: type (ALL=bad, ref/range=good, const=best), key (index used), rows (estimated rows scanned), Extra (Using filesort, Using temporary = expensive). Slow queries are the #1 cause of application latency. The query optimizer's decisions can be understood and influenced through index design, statistics, and query rewriting.
topics.story: A slow query is like a librarian searching for a book by reading every page of every book in the library. EXPLAIN is the detective tool: it shows you which books the librarian is reading, in what order, and why. Once you see 'type: ALL, rows: 5000000', you know the librarian is reading everything. Add the right index and suddenly it's 'type: ref, rows: 3' — same result, 1.6 million times less work.
topics.analogy: EXPLAIN output: type column = search strategy (ALL=full scan, index=full index scan, range=range scan, ref=index lookup, const=single row by PK/unique). key = which index was chosen (NULL = no index). rows = estimated rows MySQL will scan. Extra = 'Using filesort'=sort needs temp work, 'Using temporary'=temp table created, 'Using index'=covering index (best). Optimizer = chess grandmaster: it picks the plan it thinks is cheapest based on statistics.
topics.memoryAnchor: EXPLAIN SELECT ... → execution plan. type: const > eq_ref > ref > range > index > ALL. key: NULL = no index used. rows: lower = better. Extra: 'Using index' = covering (best). 'Using filesort' = ORDER BY not served by index. 'Using temporary' = GROUP BY/DISTINCT needs temp table. ANALYZE TABLE: updates statistics. SHOW INDEX FROM table: see all indexes + cardinality. SELECT * kills covering index optimization. Force index: USE INDEX(idx_name).
topics.firstPrinciples: MySQL optimizer is cost-based: it estimates I/O cost of each plan using table statistics (cardinality, row count). Low cardinality columns (status='active' for 90% of rows) may cause the optimizer to prefer full scan over index. EXPLAIN ANALYZE (MySQL 8.0.18+) runs the query and shows actual vs estimated rows — catches bad optimizer estimates. The optimizer can use at most one index per table per JOIN step (usually).
topics.bruteForce: Write SELECT * everywhere — kills covering index. Use functions on indexed columns: WHERE YEAR(created_at)=2024. Implicit type conversions: WHERE user_id='123' (user_id is INT). No composite index: separate indexes that can't combine for range queries. SELECT without LIMIT on large tables.
topics.optimizedApproach: SELECT only needed columns. Index columns in WHERE, JOIN, ORDER BY, GROUP BY. Composite index order: equality columns first, range columns last. Covering index: SELECT cols all in index. Rewrite functions as ranges. EXPLAIN every query touching > 10k rows. Use pt-query-digest or slow_query_log to find production slow queries. ANALYZE TABLE after bulk inserts.
topics.whenToUse: Run EXPLAIN: before deploying any new query on tables >10k rows. Profile with slow_query_log (log_queries_not_using_indexes=ON). Optimize when: EXPLAIN shows type=ALL on large table, rows estimate >> actual matches, Extra shows 'Using filesort' on frequently run queries. Don't over-index: each index slows writes by ~5–15ms per DML.

examples.title: Reading EXPLAIN Output & Common Anti-Patterns
examples.code:
-- ── Setup ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
CREATE TABLE submissions (
    id         BIGINT PRIMARY KEY AUTO_INCREMENT,
    user_id    BIGINT NOT NULL,
    topic_id   BIGINT NOT NULL,
    status     VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL,
    created_at DATETIME NOT NULL,
    score      INT
);
INSERT INTO submissions SELECT ... -- 2 million rows

-- ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
-- ANTI-PATTERN 1: Function on indexed column
-- ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
-- BAD:
EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM submissions WHERE YEAR(created_at) = 2024;
-- type: ALL, rows: 2000000 — function destroys index use

-- GOOD: range on column directly
EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM submissions
    WHERE created_at >= '2024-01-01' AND created_at < '2025-01-01';
-- type: range, key: idx_created (if exists), rows: ~250000

-- ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
-- ANTI-PATTERN 2: SELECT * kills covering index
-- ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
CREATE INDEX idx_user_status ON submissions(user_id, status);

-- BAD: SELECT * requires heap fetch even with index
EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM submissions WHERE user_id = 42 AND status = 'ACCEPTED';
-- key: idx_user_status, Extra: NULL (heap fetch needed for *)

-- GOOD: covering index
CREATE INDEX idx_user_status_date ON submissions(user_id, status, created_at);
EXPLAIN SELECT created_at FROM submissions WHERE user_id = 42 AND status = 'ACCEPTED';
-- key: idx_user_status_date, Extra: Using index (covering!)

-- ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
-- ANTI-PATTERN 3: Implicit type conversion
-- ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
-- BAD: user_id is BIGINT, comparing with string → full scan
EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM submissions WHERE user_id = '42'; -- may skip index!
-- GOOD:
EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM submissions WHERE user_id = 42;

-- ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
-- ANTI-PATTERN 4: OR across different columns
-- ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
-- BAD: optimizer can't use one index for OR across columns
EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM submissions WHERE user_id = 42 OR topic_id = 7;
-- type: ALL (index merge sometimes, but often full scan)

-- GOOD: UNION ALL
EXPLAIN
    SELECT * FROM submissions WHERE user_id = 42
    UNION ALL
    SELECT * FROM submissions WHERE topic_id = 7 AND user_id != 42;
-- Each branch can use its own index
examples.explanation: EXPLAIN columns to focus on: type (quality of access), key (NULL = problem), rows (magnitude matters), Extra (filesort/temporary = consider indexes or rewrite). Functions on indexed columns prevent index use — always rewrite as range conditions. SELECT * prevents covering index optimization — only SELECT columns you need. Type conversion is silent but deadly — Java ORM must match Java type to DB column type.
examples.realWorldUse: DevLearner slow queries to check: submissions by user+topic+status (needs composite index), topics by category+displayOrder (needs composite index), problems by topicId+difficulty. Run EXPLAIN ANALYZE in MySQL 8+ to see actual vs estimated rows — great for catching stale statistics after bulk seed imports.

examples.title: JOIN Optimization & Subquery vs JOIN
examples.code:
-- Setup
CREATE TABLE users   (id BIGINT PK, name VARCHAR, email VARCHAR);
CREATE TABLE topics  (id BIGINT PK, title VARCHAR, category VARCHAR);
CREATE TABLE progress(user_id BIGINT, topic_id BIGINT, stage VARCHAR, completed_at DATETIME);
-- FK indexes (InnoDB doesn't auto-index FKs referenced, only declaring side)
CREATE INDEX idx_progress_user    ON progress(user_id);
CREATE INDEX idx_progress_topic   ON progress(topic_id);
CREATE INDEX idx_progress_user_topic ON progress(user_id, topic_id);

-- ── EXPLAIN a JOIN ────────────────────────────────────────────────
EXPLAIN
SELECT u.name, t.title, p.stage
FROM progress p
JOIN users  u ON u.id = p.user_id
JOIN topics t ON t.id = p.topic_id
WHERE p.stage = 'MASTERED'
ORDER BY p.completed_at DESC
LIMIT 20;
-- progress: type=ref on idx that includes stage? or ALL if no stage index
-- JOIN order: MySQL optimizer may reorder JOINs for efficiency

-- ── Subquery vs JOIN ─────────────────────────────────────────────
-- BAD: correlated subquery — runs once per row in outer query
SELECT u.name,
    (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM submissions s WHERE s.user_id = u.id) AS sub_count
FROM users u;
-- O(users * log(submissions)) — could be slow for 100k users

-- GOOD: JOIN with aggregation
SELECT u.name, COUNT(s.id) AS sub_count
FROM users u
LEFT JOIN submissions s ON s.user_id = u.id
GROUP BY u.id, u.name;
-- single scan of submissions with GROUP BY

-- ── IN vs EXISTS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
-- IN with subquery: materializes entire subquery result
SELECT * FROM users WHERE id IN (SELECT user_id FROM submissions WHERE status='ACCEPTED');

-- EXISTS: stops at first match per row — better when subquery is large
SELECT * FROM users u
WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM submissions s WHERE s.user_id = u.id AND s.status='ACCEPTED');

-- Modern MySQL (8.0) often optimizes IN to semijoin = same as EXISTS
-- Use EXPLAIN to verify which plan MySQL chooses

-- ── LIMIT with ORDER BY ───────────────────────────────────────────
-- BAD: sort all rows then take 20
SELECT * FROM submissions ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 20;
-- Extra: Using filesort (if no index on created_at)

-- GOOD: index covers ORDER BY
CREATE INDEX idx_created ON submissions(created_at DESC);
EXPLAIN SELECT id, user_id, status FROM submissions ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 20;
-- type: index (index scan in order), no filesort, rows: 20
examples.explanation: JOIN order: MySQL picks the join order with minimum estimated rows for the first table. Correlated subquery executes N times (once per outer row) — almost always replaceable with a JOIN+aggregation. IN vs EXISTS: in MySQL 8, the optimizer often treats them identically via semijoin transformation. Verify with EXPLAIN. LIMIT+ORDER BY: the most efficient pattern is an index that matches the ORDER BY column and direction — zero sort overhead.
examples.realWorldUse: DevLearner: leaderboard query (top N users by XP) — needs index on users(xp DESC). User progress dashboard — LEFT JOIN topics + progress with covering index. Admin: 'users with most submissions' — JOIN + GROUP BY + ORDER BY COUNT DESC LIMIT 10. Always add LIMIT in admin queries — prevent accidental full-table dumps.

problems.title: Identify the Slow Query Problem
problems.description:
Optimize a slow query using EXPLAIN and index hints.

**Slow query:**
```sql
SELECT * FROM orders o
JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.id
WHERE c.country = 'US' AND o.status = 'PENDING'
ORDER BY o.created_at;
```

**Steps:**
1. Run `EXPLAIN` to find full table scans
2. Add `INDEX(country)` on customers, `INDEX(status, created_at)` on orders
3. Verify with `EXPLAIN` again: `type` should change from `ALL` to `ref`

**Key insight:** Look for `type=ALL` and `rows` estimate in EXPLAIN. Composite index on (status, created_at) allows both filtering and sorting.
problems.hint1: type=ALL + key=NULL = full table scan. Find why index isn't used.
problems.hint2: MONTH(created_at) is a function — prevents index use. Rewrite as range.
problems.hint3: SELECT * prevents covering index. Add composite index on (status, created_at, amount).
problems.starterCode:
-- Given slow query:
SELECT * FROM orders
WHERE MONTH(created_at) = 3
  AND status = 'PENDING'
ORDER BY amount DESC;

-- EXPLAIN shows: type=ALL, key=NULL, rows=5000000, Extra='Using where; Using filesort'

-- Q1: What are the 3 problems?
-- Answer:

-- Q2: Rewrite the query to be index-friendly
-- Answer:

-- Q3: What index would you create?
-- Answer:
problems.solutionCode:
-- Q1: Three problems:
-- 1. MONTH(created_at) → function on column → index cannot be used
-- 2. No composite index for (status, created_at) → full table scan
-- 3. SELECT * → covering index impossible → heap fetch for every row

-- Q2: Rewrite (assuming March 2024 is intended, use parameter)
SELECT id, user_id, amount, created_at FROM orders
WHERE status = 'PENDING'
  AND created_at >= '2024-03-01'
  AND created_at  < '2024-04-01'
ORDER BY amount DESC;
-- Equality on status first (high selectivity), range on created_at, no function

-- Q3: Composite covering index
CREATE INDEX idx_status_created_amount
    ON orders(status, created_at, amount);
-- WHERE status='PENDING' (equality) → range on created_at → amount for ORDER BY
-- EXPLAIN should show: type=range, key=idx_status_created_amount, Extra='Using index'
problems.testCases:
Index: idx_status_created_amount on (status, created_at, amount)
Query uses range scan not full scan
No filesort with covering index
problems.sampleInput: EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM orders JOIN customers ON ... WHERE country='US' AND status='PENDING'
problems.sampleOutput: After index: type=ref, rows reduced from 100000 to ~500

problems.title: Rewrite Correlated Subquery as JOIN
problems.description:
Rewrite a correlated subquery as a JOIN for better performance.

**Correlated subquery (slow — runs once per row):**
```sql
SELECT * FROM employees e
WHERE salary > (SELECT AVG(salary) FROM employees WHERE department_id = e.department_id);
```

**Optimized with JOIN:**
```sql
SELECT e.* FROM employees e
JOIN (SELECT department_id, AVG(salary) avg_sal FROM employees GROUP BY department_id) dept_avg
ON e.department_id = dept_avg.department_id
WHERE e.salary > dept_avg.avg_sal;
```

**Key insight:** Correlated subquery executes once per row = O(n²). Derived table pre-computes all dept averages once = O(n).
problems.pattern: Correlated subquery to JOIN, GROUP BY
problems.hint1: Replace the subquery with a LEFT JOIN to submissions filtered by status.
problems.hint2: Use COUNT(s.id) — returns 0 for users with no submissions (LEFT JOIN returns NULL, COUNT ignores NULL).
problems.hint3: GROUP BY u.id to aggregate. WHERE on users stays as-is.
problems.starterCode:
-- Original (slow — correlated subquery):
SELECT u.name,
    (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM submissions s WHERE s.user_id = u.id AND s.status='ACCEPTED') AS solved
FROM users u
WHERE u.active = 1;

-- Rewrite as JOIN:
-- Answer:
problems.solutionCode:
-- Rewritten as LEFT JOIN + GROUP BY:
SELECT u.name, COUNT(s.id) AS solved
FROM users u
LEFT JOIN submissions s
    ON s.user_id = u.id
    AND s.status = 'ACCEPTED'   -- filter BEFORE join (not in WHERE)
WHERE u.active = 1
GROUP BY u.id, u.name
ORDER BY solved DESC;

-- Why LEFT JOIN: users with 0 solved submissions still appear (COUNT = 0)
-- COUNT(s.id) not COUNT(*): NULLs from LEFT JOIN are ignored by COUNT(col)
-- Filter s.status in ON clause (not WHERE) to keep users with 0 matches
problems.testCases: Returns all active users with their accepted submission count (0 if none)
problems.sampleInput: Correlated subquery comparing salary to dept average
problems.sampleOutput: Rewritten as JOIN with derived table — single scan instead of N scans

problems.title: Pagination Query Optimization
problems.description:
Use query cache and pagination best practices to optimize paginated queries.

**Slow pagination:**
```sql
SELECT * FROM products ORDER BY id LIMIT 100 OFFSET 1000000;
```

**Problem:** MySQL scans and discards 1M rows.

**Fast keyset pagination:**
```sql
SELECT * FROM products WHERE id > :last_seen_id ORDER BY id LIMIT 100;
```

**Key insight:** `OFFSET` forces a full scan up to the offset position. Keyset pagination uses the index directly — O(1) regardless of page depth.
problems.hint1: OFFSET N scans and discards N rows before returning results — O(N) work for each page.
problems.hint2: Keyset: track the last seen id. Next page: WHERE id > :lastId ORDER BY id LIMIT 20.
problems.hint3: Keyset uses the PK index directly — O(log n + page_size) regardless of depth.
problems.starterCode:
-- SLOW: deep pagination
SELECT * FROM problems ORDER BY id LIMIT 20 OFFSET 100000;
-- Problem: MySQL scans 100020 rows, discards 100000

-- Q1: Why is OFFSET slow?
-- Answer:

-- Q2: Rewrite using cursor-based pagination (assume lastSeenId = 5000)
-- Answer:

-- Q3: What is the complexity difference?
-- Answer:
problems.solutionCode:
-- Q1: Why OFFSET is slow:
-- MySQL must scan and discard OFFSET rows before returning LIMIT rows.
-- OFFSET 100000 → reads 100020 rows, returns 20. Gets slower linearly with offset.

-- Q2: Cursor-based pagination (keyset)
-- First page:
SELECT id, title, difficulty FROM problems
ORDER BY id
LIMIT 20;
-- Returns ids 1..20. Save last id = 20.

-- Next page (lastSeenId = 20):
SELECT id, title, difficulty FROM problems
WHERE id > 20          -- cursor: start after last seen
ORDER BY id
LIMIT 20;
-- Uses PK index: O(log n) seek + 20 reads — same cost regardless of page depth

-- Q3: Complexity comparison:
-- OFFSET: O(offset + limit) — page 5000 is 100x slower than page 1
-- Keyset: O(log n + limit) — constant regardless of depth
-- Trade-off: keyset cannot jump to arbitrary page (no 'go to page 500')
problems.testCases: Keyset pagination is O(log n + limit) vs OFFSET O(offset + limit)
problems.sampleOutput: Rewrite as: WHERE id > last_seen_id LIMIT 100 (O(1) vs O(n))
